Three-Lens Framework
Framework Risks Mitigations Assess Resources Download XLSX

Resources

Case Studies

Three fictional but plausible deployment scenarios based on AIRI incident patterns.

Fintech — Shadow Agents and Customer Fraud

A mid-size fintech deployed 12 customer service agents. PL4 (inventory) discovered 23 — 11 unauthorized agents across marketing, sales, and engineering. A 4-hour red-team exercise found a vulnerability that would have cost $12,000+ per incident. Six months post-deployment: zero agent-enabled fraud incidents, confabulation rate down 35%, maturity score improved from 3.8 to 2.1.

Healthcare — Discriminatory Performance Before Patient Harm

A regional healthcare provider's clinical decision support agent showed a 9-point diagnostic accuracy gap between white and Black patients. The bias was caught during assessment using OP-MD2 scoring methodology — not after patient harm. Post-mitigation: gap reduced to 3 points. No adverse outcomes attributed to discriminatory agent behavior.

Government — Systemic Risk Disclosure Before Public Launch

A national agency published a Systemic Impact Statement 3 months before launching a citizen-facing AI agent serving 2 million people. The disclosure defused pre-launch criticism from civil society organizations and was cited in parliamentary oversight as an example of proactive AI governance. The template was adopted by two other agencies.

Limitations

The framework is an expert synthesis of 43+ taxonomies — not a systematic review. The 44 mitigation techniques have not been empirically validated. The First Five deployment sequence is based on mapping matrix coverage, not operational evidence.

The framework focuses on enterprise AI agent deployments and does not address autonomous weapons, state-level AI competition, or AI welfare. Systemic risks (the SY axis) have partial mitigations — the EX group provides leverage but does not claim complete coverage.

Severity tiers are based on AIRI incident data as of 2026. Emerging risks have near-zero current incidents but potentially catastrophic trajectories. Tiers should be revisited as incident data accumulates.

References

Man, J. (2022). Towards the Future of Work: Managing the Risks of AI and Automation. MIT Sloan School of Management.

Slattery, P., et al. (2024). The AI Risk Repository. arXiv:2408.12622.

OWASP. (2025). OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications.

OWASP. (2026). OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications.

NIST. (2023). AI Risk Management Framework 1.0. NIST AI 100-1.

NIST. (2024). GenAI Profile. NIST AI 600-1.

European Union. (2024). AI Act. Regulation 2024/1689.

ISO/IEC. (2023). ISO/IEC 42001:2023.

Full reference list (27+ academic publications) available in the framework document.

About

The Three-Lens Framework extends James Man's 2022 MIT thesis with the MIT AI Risk Repository (1,366 incidents, 1,700+ risks), OWASP Agentic Top 10 (2026), NIST AI RMF 1.0 & GenAI Profile (2023/2024), EU AI Act (2024), ISO/IEC 42001:2023, and 27+ academic publications on agentic AI governance (2023-2026).

Three-Lens Framework

An AI agent risk assessment framework for enterprise deployments. 87 risks across 11 categories. Grounded in 1,366 real-world incidents.

Based On

Man, J. (2022). Towards the Future of Work: Managing the Risks of AI and Automation. MIT Sloan School of Management.

Extended with MIT AIRI, OWASP LLM & Agentic Top 10, NIST AI RMF, EU AI Act, ISO/IEC 42001, and 27+ academic publications.
